aliases: Laksa Sarawak (砂拉越叻沙; Kuching laksa)
East Malaysian coconut-prawn paste noodle soup

english: East Malaysian coconut-prawn paste noodle soup
chinese: Laksa Sarawak (砂拉越叻沙; Kuching laksa)

region_of_origin: Malaysia — Sarawak, Borneo (associated with Kuching, the capital of Sarawak)
food_subgroup: Noodle soup — rice vermicelli in thin coconut milk broth with sambal belacan, shredded chicken, prawns, and fried egg strips

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Laksa Sarawak specifically. Closest parent concept: rice noodle soup with coconut milk and shrimp paste broth (FOODON:03315595 — noodle soup — used as closest parent only; not an exact match).
ontology.foodex2_description: Laksa Sarawak — rice vermicelli in thin coconut milk broth with sambal belacan (fermented shrimp paste, chilli, lemongrass, galangal, tamarind), shredded chicken, prawns, and fried egg strips
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Laksa Sarawak. Closest parent: composite rice noodle dish with coconut milk broth. Code not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Laksa Sarawak as a specific food concept.

main_ingredients: sambal belacan (fermented shrimp paste, chilli, lemongrass, galangal, tamarind)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Laksa Sarawak weighs approximately 460g, comprising roughly 150g rice vermicelli, 260g thin coconut milk broth, and 50g of garnishes including shredded chicken, prawns, fried egg strips, bean sprouts, and coriander.

nutrient_source.title: Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD) — Laksa Sarawak / composite noodle soup with coconut milk and shrimp paste
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Ministry of Health Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Laksa Sarawak (composite dish). Values represent the full assembled dish including broth, noodles, and garnishes. Where specific micronutrient values were not directly available in MyFCD, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (chicken, prawns, coconut milk, rice vermicelli) was used to cross-validate and supplement. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported in MyFCD for this composite dish; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Vermicelli and Coconut Milk Noodle Soups — analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI (58–65) from University of Sydney GI Database; thin coconut milk fat content modestly moderates digestion rate relative to plain water broth
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Laksa Sarawak in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) rice vermicelli GI range of 58–65 as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodle products; (2) thin coconut milk fat content (approximately 4.5g fat per 100g dish) providing modest slowing of gastric emptying relative to plain broth; (3) GL per 460g bowl calculated as (62 × 55.2g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 34.2 (HIGH). This estimate is consistent with the input record's IMR Malaysia MyFCD provenance note.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 3,128mg per 460g bowl, primarily from sambal belacan (fermented shrimp paste) and seasoned coconut broth, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Laksa Sarawak; if consumed, request reduced belacan paste and consume only half the broth to reduce sodium intake to approximately 1,500–1,800mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load of approximately 34 per standard 460g bowl driven by 150g rice vermicelli (GI ~62); despite thin coconut milk fat providing modest digestion-rate moderation, the overall GL remains clinically high.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half noodle volume (approximately 75g vermicelli), which reduces GL to approximately 17 (moderate range), and pair with additional protein from chicken and prawns to further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (2.8g per 100g; approximately 12.9g per bowl) and high sodium (3,128mg per bowl) are the primary cardiovascular concerns; however, fat content is lower than Peninsular Malaysian laksa variants due to thinner broth.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Laksa Sarawak occasionally and in reduced portions, prioritising the protein components (chicken, prawns) while limiting broth consumption to reduce both saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laksa Sarawak (砂拉越叻沙) is the iconic noodle soup of Sarawak, the largest state of Malaysia on the island of Borneo, and is most closely associated with Kuching, the Sarawak state capital. It is distinct from Peninsular Malaysian laksa variants in using a thinner, lighter coconut milk broth flavoured with a unique sambal belacan paste made from fermented dried shrimp (belacan), fresh and dried chillies, lemongrass, galangal, and tamarind — producing a complex, aromatic, mildly spicy, and slightly sour broth. The dish is served over fine rice vermicelli and garnished with shredded poached chicken, whole prawns, fried egg strips (a distinctive Sarawak garnish), fresh bean sprouts, and coriander leaves. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. A standard 460g bowl provides approximately 497 kcal, 25.3g protein, 20.7g fat (12.9g saturated from coconut milk), 55.2g carbohydrate, and 3.7g dietary fibre. Sodium is very high at approximately 3,128mg per bowl, dominated by the sambal belacan paste. GI is approximately 62 (medium) with a high glycaemic load of approximately 34 per bowl. Clinically, the primary concerns are very high sodium (hypertension risk), high GL (Type 2 Diabetes management), and moderate saturated fat (cardiovascular risk). The dish is lower in fat and calories than Singapore laksa or Penang curry laksa due to its thinner broth. Famously described by Anthony Bourdain as the 'breakfast of the gods', Laksa Sarawak reflects the multicultural Malay, Iban, Chinese, and Peranakan heritage of Sarawak. Source: IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research, Ministry of Health Malaysia).

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research, Ministry of Health Malaysia), which is the primary approved regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values are internally consistent: energy check = (5.5 × 4) + (4.5 × 9) + (12.0 × 4) = 22 + 40.5 + 48 = 110.5 kcal vs reported 108 kcal (within acceptable rounding tolerance for a composite dish with water content). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli data, clearly documented. Confidence is HIGH.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Laksa Sarawak as a composite dish is not reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the assembled composite dish is not separately reported in MyFCD. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are expected in this traditional dish prepared without hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per MyFCD. 4. tamil and hindi local_names are null: Laksa Sarawak is a Malaysian Sarawakian dish, not a Singaporean dish; Tamil and Hindi mandatory multilingual requirements apply to Singaporean dishes only under G6 rules. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI range (58–65) from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Laksa Sarawak exists in the database. GL per 460g bowl = (62 × 55.2) / 100 = 34.2 (HIGH). 6. Sodium of 3,128mg per bowl is calculated as 680mg per 100g × 4.6 (460g serving). 7. The hypertension health_context notes suitable:true in the input record despite very high sodium — this has been corrected to concern_level:high in the output, as 3,128mg sodium per bowl clearly exceeds safe limits for hypertensive patients.
